title_en: The Relationship Between Bid'ah and the Objectives of the Sharia
content_en:
One of al-Shatibi's most original contributions in Al-I'tisam is his analysis of bid'ah through the lens of the objectives (maqasid) of Islamic law — the framework he had developed more fully in his other masterwork, Al-Muwafaqat. This approach allows him to ground his opposition to bid'ah not merely in specific prophetic prohibitions but in a comprehensive understanding of what Islamic law is designed to achieve and how religious innovation subverts those objectives.

The objectives of the Sharia, as al-Shatibi classifies them in Al-Muwafaqat, are the preservation of five essential interests: religion (din), life (nafs), intellect ('aql), lineage (nasl), and wealth (mal). The Sharia's rules are designed, at their deepest level, to protect and promote these five interests for every individual and for the community as a whole. Religious innovation, in al-Shatibi's analysis, threatens primarily the first of these interests: the preservation of the religion itself.

The preservation of religion requires both that the authentic teachings of Islam be protected from distortion and that the practice of Islam be aligned with the divine guidance revealed to the Prophet. Bid'ah threatens both of these requirements. It threatens the first by introducing new 'religious' teachings that have no basis in the Quran or Sunnah, thereby contaminating the authentic body of Islamic knowledge with human inventions. It threatens the second by replacing or supplementing prophetically authorized forms of worship with humanly devised alternatives, thereby distorting the practice of the religion as the Prophet left it.

Al-Shatibi also argues that bid'ah undermines the internal logic of the Sharia. Islamic law is not an arbitrary collection of rules but an internally coherent system whose parts are designed to work together to achieve the divine objectives. The specific forms of worship commanded by the Sharia — the five prayers, the fast of Ramadan, the zakat, the hajj — are not arbitrary but are designed by divine wisdom to achieve specific spiritual and social effects in the life of the believer and the community. When a person supplements these with invented forms, he implicitly treats the Sharia as incomplete or insufficient — as if the divinely designed system needs human improvement. This is a fundamental misunderstanding of the nature of revelation.

Furthermore, religious innovation disrupts the social and communal dimension of Islamic practice. The uniformity of prophetically authorized practice creates a bond of unity among Muslims across time and geography: all Muslims pray in essentially the same way the Prophet prayed, fast the same month he fasted, perform the same pilgrimage he performed. This uniformity is itself a social and spiritual good, reflecting the community's collective submission to a single divine authority. Religious innovations, by contrast, introduce variations that fracture this uniformity and create competing claims about what constitutes valid religious practice. The history of Islamic sects and their disputes often traces back to divergent innovations in religious practice.

Al-Shatibi's maqasid-based analysis of bid'ah demonstrates the methodological sophistication that distinguishes his work from more straightforward compilations of anti-innovation hadith. By grounding the prohibition of bid'ah in the objectives of the Sharia, he shows that it is not an arbitrary conservatism but a principled fidelity to the divine design embedded in the Islamic legal and spiritual tradition. This approach has been enormously influential on subsequent scholarship, particularly in the Maliki tradition and in the broader movement of Islamic legal reform that sought to reconnect Islamic practice with the deeper purposes of divine guidance.
content_ar: البدعة تتعارض مع مقاصد الشريعة لأن الشريعة جاءت كاملة، والبدعة تقتضي أن ثمة نقصاً في الدين يحتاج إلى استدراك.
